summary: Card sorting is a UX research method where participants organize labeled cards into groups, revealing mental models for intuitive IA. Three types: Open (users create groups), Closed (predefined categories), Hybrid (mix). Sample sizes: 15 for qualitative, 30-50+ for quantitative. Analysis via similarity matrices and dendrograms. Optimal card count: 30-50. Limitations: lacks contextual cues, reveals only single categorization level.
tactics: Use open card sorting to discover natural user categorizations before designing IA
Target 30-50 cards per session to prevent fatigue
Require 30+ participants for statistically valid quantitative patterns
Combine quantitative patterns with moderated sessions for reasoning insights
Use similarity matrices and dendrograms to identify hierarchical groupings

summary: Tree testing evaluates findability of items in a given navigation structure. Users are shown text-only site hierarchy and asked where they'd click to accomplish goals. Recommended workflow: tree test existing IA to assess → card sort to understand user mental models → tree test redesigned IA to compare. Complements card sorting as evaluation vs generation method.
tactics: Use tree testing to evaluate existing IA before redesigning
Run tree test → card sort → tree test cycle for IA redesigns
Analyze success rate, first click accuracy, and directness metrics
Test with text-only hierarchy to isolate structure from visual design

topic: Mental Models and Cognitive Load in Navigation
summary: Cognitive load theory (Sweller, 1980s) posits limited human cognitive capacity; overload decreases performance. Navigation is critical — poorly designed navigation causes high cognitive load, frustration, and abandonment. Measurement methods: error rates, time-on-task, correct answers. Clear, well-structured navigation reduces mental effort. Users bring mental models from past experiences to new interfaces.
tactics: Design navigation matching users' existing mental models from competitor products
Measure cognitive load via error rates and time-on-task metrics
Reduce extraneous cognitive load by eliminating unnecessary UI elements
Use familiar patterns (tabs, sidebars) that leverage existing mental models
Limit navigation choices to 5-7 primary items per level

summary: Progressive disclosure defers advanced features to secondary screens. Two-level structure works best — primary features initially, specialized on request. Benefits: improved learnability (novices focus on essentials), efficiency (experts avoid scanning), reduced errors. IBM lab work (Carroll & Rosson, 1980s) showed hiding advanced features early increased later success. Interfaces achieve 30-50% faster initial task completion while maintaining 70-90% feature discoverability. Beyond 2 levels causes navigation confusion.
tactics: Limit progressive disclosure to exactly 2 levels — primary and secondary
Use task analysis and frequency-of-use data to determine feature placement
Ensure clear information scent on triggers — users must know what expanding will reveal
Distinguish from staged disclosure (wizards) which is linear vs hierarchical
Base progressive disclosure decisions on observed user behavior not assumptions

topic: Navigation Patterns: Flat vs Deep, Hub-and-Spoke, Faceted
summary: Hub-and-spoke: users navigate from central hub to detail pages and return — elegant, easy to learn, flat architecture. Linear navigation: call-and-return, intervening page flow. Faceted navigation: leverages metadata for refining queries, works better with broad shallow taxonomies vs deep drill-down. Flat/shallow taxonomies generally preferred over deep hierarchies. Well-designed navigation improves task completion by 37%.
tactics: Use hub-and-spoke for mobile apps with 3-5 top-level sections
Prefer shallow broad taxonomy over deep hierarchy to reduce drill-down fatigue
Implement faceted navigation for content-heavy apps with multiple filter dimensions
Reserve linear navigation for sequential task flows (checkout, onboarding)
Test navigation with task completion rate as primary metric

summary: Five main onboarding patterns: (1) Checklists — show completed/undone steps, prevent users getting lost; (2) Tooltips — action-driven, drive attention to features, good for complex setups; (3) Product Tours — broad overview via screens/videos, high-level understanding; (4) Progressive Onboarding — reveals features as users gain familiarity; (5) Empty-State-Driven — blank canvases that educate, nudge, and guide. HubSpot checklist: 15% retention lift week 1, 50% increase after 10 weeks. Users completing onboarding 38% more likely to return after 1 week.
tactics: Use checklists for multi-step setups requiring sequential completion tracking
Deploy tooltips for action-driven guidance on specific features
Reserve product tours for initial broad overview only — not detailed training
Implement progressive onboarding for complex products with many features
Design empty states as onboarding opportunities with CTAs and educational content
Track activation rate, time-to-value, and feature discovery rate as onboarding KPIs

summary: PLG onboarding key metrics: NPS (50%), Activation (42%), Time to Initial Value (42%), Key Feature Adoption (33%), Usage (25%), Team Activation (25%). Freemium converts at 12% — 140% higher than free trials. Automated onboarding reduces time by 30-50%. 52% of customers say quick onboarding influences loyalty. Design for 'aha moment' — the earliest point users realize product value.
tactics: Design onboarding to minimize Time to Initial Value as primary metric
Target activation rate as core conversion signal in PLG
Use automated onboarding flows to reduce manual setup by 30-50%
Design for the 'aha moment' — identify and accelerate path to first value realization
Monitor freemium-to-paid conversion as ultimate onboarding effectiveness measure

summary: Wizards break complex processes into sequential manageable steps. Best for: onboarding, complex forms, multi-stage tasks. Avoid for: short simple tasks, artificially stretched processes. Optimal step count: 3-7. Branching logic shows/hides steps based on previous inputs creating perceived linear flow. Inline validation with immediate feedback essential. Backward navigation must preserve progress. Mobile: larger tap targets, minimal info per step, touch-friendly navigation.
tactics: Keep wizard steps between 3-7 to balance complexity with completion motivation
Implement branching logic to personalize flow based on user inputs
Always allow backward navigation without losing entered data
Use inline validation with actionable error messages not generic warnings
Show numbered progress indicators with remaining step count
Optimize for mobile with larger tap targets and minimal per-step content

summary: Command palettes originated from Apple Spotlight (2005). Solve scalability limitations of graphical interfaces — reduce visual clutter in complex apps. Combine fuzzy search, universal content search, keyboard-first workflows. Three variations: universal launcher (navigation+creation+settings), contextual palette (current object actions), recent-first (repeat commands prioritized). Implementation: cmdk (unstyled composable), react-cmdk (pre-styled), cmdk-engine (route discovery, RBAC, frecency). Accessibility: semantic HTML first, ARIA additions, keyboard-only verification, 200% zoom testing.
tactics: Implement Cmd+K as primary power-user navigation for apps with 50+ actions
Use fuzzy search with synonym support — users rarely remember exact command names
Categorize results into groups: navigation, actions, recent, settings
Show keyboard shortcuts inline within palette results for progressive learning
Support three palette types: universal launcher, contextual, and recent-first
Use cmdk library for unstyled composable implementation with React

summary: Three breadcrumb types: Location-based (hierarchical position), Attribute-based (applied filters), Path-based (browsing history). Use when: 3+ levels deep, users land from search engines, context clarity needed. Skip when: flat structure (1-2 levels), linear workflows (use steppers). Mobile patterns: parent-only link, horizontal scrollable bar, collapsed dropdown. Truncation strategies: start, middle, tail, collapsed with ellipsis. Place below global nav, above page title. Chevrons (›) preferred over slashes.
tactics: Use location-based breadcrumbs for hierarchical sites with 3+ depth levels
Skip breadcrumbs for flat structures — use 'Back to [Parent]' links instead
On mobile use parent-only breadcrumb pattern to save space
Use chevrons (›) as separators not slashes — clearer directional flow
Position below global navigation, above page title consistently
Implement attribute-based breadcrumbs for ecommerce filter scenarios

summary: Navigation causes 30-40% of mobile usability problems. Users who can't find content within 10-15 seconds abandon. Tab bars work best for 3-5 primary destinations in thumb-friendly zone. Hamburger menus: reserve for secondary/infrequent items only. Bottom sheets for frequently-used functions. Gestures: speed for experts but discoverability challenge — keep visible controls for core actions. Sidebar: replace with bottom tab bar on mobile (up to 5 items) or full-screen slide-over drawer.
tactics: Limit tab bar to 3-5 primary destinations maximum
Place most frequent actions in lower screen half for thumb accessibility
Never bury high-frequency actions in hamburger menus
Use gestures as optional accelerators not primary navigation
Replace desktop sidebar with bottom tab bar or drawer on mobile
Redesign navigation when users rely on search for basic features

summary: Dashboard design starts with user intent — what must be understood in 5 seconds? KPIs and summaries at top, granular data in expandable sections (progressive disclosure). Widget customization: users rearrange, add, remove widgets per role. AI-powered personalization: dashboards learn user preferences. Group related metrics together (e.g., Campaign Performance section). Minimize clutter — minimalist trend continues in 2026. Alert management: reserve alerts for critical events only. Accessibility: high contrast, keyboard nav, screen reader support.
tactics: Design for 5-second comprehension — place primary KPI at top-left
Use progressive disclosure: summaries visible, details expandable
Allow widget customization per user role — rearrange, add, remove
Group related metrics into logical sections with clear headings
Reserve alerts/notifications for critical events only to prevent fatigue
Implement consistent data formatting: scales, time intervals, currency across all widgets

summary: Five error types: User-generated (invalid input), Environmental (network/permissions), System (bugs/API failures), Security/Permission (expired sessions), Business Logic (rule constraints). Nielsen's 3-step recovery: Recognize → Diagnose → Recover. Slips (correct intent, wrong execution) vs Mistakes (wrong intent). Inline validation after submission not on blur. Smart defaults and input masks prevent errors. Session expiry: tell users how long until retry, provide recovery path. Network errors: preserve progress with local drafts, enable 'Resume' not 'Restart'. Graceful degradation with offline mode indicators.
tactics: Classify errors into 5 types and design specific recovery flows for each
Implement inline validation on form submission — not on field blur to avoid premature feedback
Preserve user progress with local drafts during network failures
Provide 'Resume' option not 'Restart' after session expiry or network loss
Use smart defaults and input masks to prevent errors proactively
Design fail-safe backends: retry failed steps, degrade gracefully, surface actionable messages

summary: Error pages must reduce stress and show recovery paths. Every 404 needs: homepage button, product/service links, site search. Use 'mistake' instead of 'error' to reduce emotional impact. Clean focused design around single goal: get user back on track. Maintain brand consistency with main site. Permission errors: clarity about why access is denied plus next steps. Avoid unnecessary text, busy graphics, long explanations.
tactics: Include search, homepage link, and popular page links on every 404 page
Use empathetic language — 'mistake' instead of 'error' reduces user stress
Keep error pages clean and focused on single recovery goal
Maintain full brand consistency — error pages should look like the main site
For permission errors explain why access is denied and provide next steps
Design error pages as opportunities not dead ends — suggest related content

summary: Three empty state types: No-Data (first-use), User-Action (search no results, completion confirmation), Error Management (data exists but can't display). Anatomy: Image (optional), Title (concise positive statement), Body (next actions explanation), Primary Action (button/link), Secondary CTA (optional docs link). Title framing: 'Start by adding data assets' vs 'You don't have any data assets'. In-depth alternatives for first-use: in-line documentation, onboarding tours, starter content (pre-built samples). Accessibility: decorative images need empty alt attributes.
tactics: Design three distinct empty state types: no-data, user-action, error-management
Frame titles positively: 'Start by adding...' not 'You don't have...'
Include primary CTA button in every empty state — never leave dead ends
Use starter content (pre-built samples) for complex features requiring exploration
Consider text-only empty states when multiple appear simultaneously to reduce visual fatigue
Always include backup basic empty state even when using starter content

summary: Notification types: user-generated, push, context-generated, system-generated, action-required, passive, smart. Channels: in-app (real-time badges/feeds), push (OS-level via APNs/FCM), email, SMS. Badge counts for glanceable non-interrupting info. Notification center: dedicated screen or flyout for all notifications. Inbox messages for: updates to reference later, non-urgent activity, status changes, historical record. Clear badges on app open. Synchronize badge count with total messages. User preferences critical for reducing fatigue.
tactics: Categorize notifications by urgency: action-required vs informational vs passive
Use badges for glanceable info that doesn't interrupt — clear on app open
Implement notification center as single location for all notification types
Use inbox pattern for persistent browsable notifications with lasting value
Synchronize badge counts with actual unread message totals
Provide granular user preferences for notification channels and frequency

summary: Layout types: single-page (limited options/mobile), tabbed (moderate groups), accordion (many categories), card layout (mobile-friendly), side navigation (extensive hierarchies), wizard (complex setup). Search in settings makes accessibility 5x better vs categories alone. Group into 4-5 top-level categories max. Prioritize frequently-used settings at top using usage data. Components: toggles (binary), checkboxes (independent selections), radio buttons (mutually exclusive), dropdowns (5-7 items max), sliders (ranges). Mobile: side nav collapses to hamburger, single-page preferred. Progressive disclosure for advanced options.
tactics: Always include search in settings — 5x improvement in findability over categories alone
Limit top-level setting categories to 4-5 maximum
Prioritize frequently-used settings at top based on usage analytics
Use card sorting to determine how users naturally group settings
Choose layout based on complexity: single-page (simple), tabbed (moderate), side-nav (extensive)
Apply progressive disclosure to hide advanced settings behind expandable sections

topic: Deep Linking and URL State Preservation
summary: URL-as-state pattern: UI components derive behavior from URL parameters, persisting through refreshes. Shareable state: any state shared between users via link (filters, tab selection, custom views). Benefits: state persists on reload, enables sharing via links, improves debugging. Back/forward buttons as state navigation tools — ignoring URL for UI state cripples them. Deep links: existing back stack replaced with synthetic back stack on deep link entry. Best for: non-sensitive data, filter configurations, view customizations.
tactics: Store shareable UI state in URL parameters: filters, tabs, view configurations
Use URL state for anything two users should see identically when sharing a link
Leverage browser history/back-forward as state navigation — don't break it
Implement synthetic back stacks for deep-linked entry points
Use libraries like nuqs or useSearchParams for React URL state management
Avoid URL state for sensitive data — passwords, tokens, PII

summary: Navigation types: tabs (quick movement between equal-importance views), bottom navigation bar (small number of top-level views), side navigation (alternative when tabs don't fit), drawer (hidden until invoked). Content grouping: logical relatable chunks showing clear relationships. Determine navigation by: identifying users, typical paths, desired actions. Material 3: panes conditionally displayed side-by-side or stacked based on screen width. Navigation bar for top-level sections, navigation drawer for secondary.
tactics: Use tabs for equal-importance views — max 5 tabs recommended
Use bottom navigation bar for 3-5 top-level mobile destinations
Reserve navigation drawer for secondary navigation hidden by default
Group content into logical chunks showing clear relationships
Design responsive navigation: side-by-side panes on wide screens, stacked on narrow
Test navigation paths against actual user workflows not assumed flows

summary: Tab bar: persistent at screen bottom for top-level sections, limited to 5 tabs on iPhone. Navigation bars for hierarchical drill-down. Modal presentations for focused tasks. 70% of users leave apps when feeling lost after 3 interactions. Keep main functions accessible within 2 taps from home. Liquid Glass (2025): subtle material variation reinforcing navigation depth. Components (tab bars, segmented controls, sidebars) provide consistent function and feedback across devices.
tactics: Limit iPhone tab bar to 5 tabs maximum
Ensure main functions reachable within 2 taps from home screen
Use modal presentations only for focused tasks requiring user decisions
Design for the 3-interaction rule — users leave if lost after 3 attempts
Provide consistent navigation behavior across device form factors
Use hierarchical navigation bars for drill-down content structures

summary: cmdk: unstyled composable command palette, React 18 required, good up to 2000-3000 items. react-cmdk: pre-styled with built-in UI and icons. cmdk-engine: auto-discovers routes, fuzzy search with synonyms, RBAC filtering, frecency ranking. better-cmdk: command palette with built-in AI chat. Key distinction: cmdk for custom styling flexibility, react-cmdk for quick implementation, cmdk-engine for production features.
tactics: Use cmdk for design-system-aligned implementations requiring custom styling
Use react-cmdk for rapid prototyping with pre-built UI
Use cmdk-engine for production apps needing route discovery and RBAC
Support up to 2000-3000 items for performant command palette experience
Require React 18+ for hooks like useId and useSyncExternalStore

summary: React Joyride: most popular (5.1k stars), tooltip prompts on DOM elements. Intro.js: established choice with React wrapper. React Shepherd: Shepherd.js wrapper, SVG backdrop with multi-element spotlight. react-tourlight: modern 2025-2026 library with spotlight overlays, keyboard support, theming. Onborda: MIT-licensed, Next.js + Tailwind + shadcn/ui integration with Framer Motion animations. Key gap: nothing modern, free, and React-native existed before react-tourlight.
tactics: Use React Joyride for most tooltip-based product tour needs
Use Onborda for Next.js + Tailwind + shadcn/ui stacks with Framer Motion
Consider react-tourlight for modern React-native tour needs
Use React Shepherd for multi-element simultaneous spotlight highlighting
Implement keyboard support and theming in all tour implementations

summary: CoreUI Stepper: custom indicators, validation, flexible layouts. KendoReact Form Wizard: combines Form and Stepper components. Material UI Stepper: numbered steps, wizard-like workflow. PrimeReact Stepper: StepperPanel elements for step encapsulation. Dedicated libraries: react-step-wizard (flexible), rhf-wizard (React Hook Form integration), react-multistep, react-form-wizard-component.
tactics: Use Material UI Stepper for Material Design aligned projects
Use rhf-wizard for React Hook Form integration in multi-step forms
Use react-step-wizard for maximum flexibility in step management
Consider CoreUI Stepper for custom indicator and validation requirements

summary: Aug 2024: tab design research — distinguishing tab types, visual clarity, content structuring. Jun 2024: menu design checklist for content and feature menus. Jan 2024: tree-testing result analysis — success rate, first click, directness metrics. Feb 2025: intranet IA patterns from 120+ Synthesis intranets — navigation pitfalls and best practices. Ongoing: IA study guides covering what IA is, how to run IA research, how to design navigation.
tactics: Apply NN/g tab design guidelines: distinguish between types, ensure visual clarity
Use menu design checklist: ensure menus serve content discovery and feature access
Analyze tree-testing with three metrics: success rate, first click accuracy, directness
Study intranet IA patterns for enterprise navigation design insights
